claimText: Nuclear fission involves splitting a larger atom into smaller ones, whereas nuclear fusion involves combining two atoms to form a heavier atom.
evidenceExcerpt: Fission occurs when a neutron slams into a larger atom, forcing it to excite and split into two smaller atoms... Fusion occurs when two atoms slam together to form a heavier atom
distractorHooks: Swapping the definitions of fission and fusion
Claiming both processes involve splitting atoms

claimText: Nuclear fusion can generate approximately four times more energy per kilogram of fuel compared to nuclear fission.
evidenceExcerpt: Fusion could generate four times more energy per kilogram of fuel than fission
examUse: Statement-based question on the energy efficiency of nuclear reactions.
distractorHooks: Fission generates more energy per kilogram than fusion
Fusion and fission generate the same amount of energy per kilogram

claimText: Unlike nuclear fission, nuclear fusion does not produce long-lived radioactive nuclear waste, yielding only helium and neutrons.
evidenceExcerpt: Fusion on the other hand does not create any long-lived radioactive nuclear waste. Only helium and neutrons.
examUse: Statement-based question on the environmental impact and byproducts of nuclear energy.
distractorHooks: Fusion produces long-lived radioactive waste like iodine and caesium
Fission produces only helium and neutrons

claimText: Nuclear fission uses heavy elements like Uranium and Plutonium as fuel, while nuclear fusion generally uses hydrogen isotopes such as Deuterium and Tritium.
evidenceExcerpt: Fission uses fuels like Uranium and Plutonium, while fusion generally uses hydrogen isotopes Deuterium (D) and Tritium (T).
examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question identifying fuels for different nuclear reactions.

claimText: Nuclear fusion requires extreme conditions to occur, such as plasma reaching temperatures of 150,000,000 °C within a tokamak device.
evidenceExcerpt: Fusion requires extreme conditions, such as plasma reaching 150,000,000 °C in a tokamak.
examUse: Statement-based question on the technological requirements of fusion reactors.
distractorHooks: Fusion can easily be achieved at room temperature
Tokamaks are primarily used for nuclear fission reactions

claimText: Net Energy Gain (NEG) in the context of commercial nuclear fusion means that the fusion process generates more energy than is consumed to initiate it.
evidenceExcerpt: Net Energy Gain (NEG) in commercial fusion implies the process generates more energy than it consumes.
examUse: Definition-based statement question on fusion energy viability.
distractorHooks: NEG implies the reactor consumes more energy than it produces
NEG is a term exclusively used for nuclear fission waste reduction
